data.title: Lufthansa Summer Cuts Test Fuel Constraints And Earnings Resilience
data.description:
• Lufthansa (XTRA:LHA) has cancelled 20,000 summer flights in response to a severe jet fuel shortage and sharply higher fuel costs.
  • The reductions coincide with broader capacity cuts across European airlines as energy supply chains are disrupted by the Iran war.
  • The schedule changes affect peak travel months, reshaping revenue planning, capacity use, and customer booking patterns.

Lufthansa is a major European network carrier with a significant long haul and intra European footprint. A cut of 20,000 summer flights is a material operational move. It comes as European airlines weigh fuel availability, cost volatility, and air traffic constraints when setting schedules. For you as an investor, these changes touch core drivers such as load factors, unit costs, and ancillary revenue potential.

Looking ahead, key questions center on how Lufthansa reallocates capacity, manages fuel procurement, and handles potential compensation and rebooking costs. The way management balances schedule reliability, pricing, and service levels in this environment may influence brand perception, customer loyalty, and the resilience of the business model for XTRA:LHA.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Deutsche Lufthansa by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Deutsche Lufthansa.

We've flagged 1 risk for Deutsche Lufthansa. See which could impact your investment.

The decision to cancel 20,000 summer flights pushes Lufthansa to treat fuel as a binding capacity constraint rather than just a cost line. Instead of flying a full schedule at any price, the group is choosing to trim frequency and potentially focus on routes and cabins where pricing power and load factors can better absorb higher jet-fuel expenses. For you, the key question is whether reduced volume is offset by higher yields, better aircraft utilization, and lower irregular operations costs compared with running a stressed network during a fuel shortage.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Deutsche Lufthansa Narrative

  • The cuts align with the narrative focus on cost discipline and network optimization, because concentrating flying on more profitable routes can support the case for a leaner, more resilient group.
  • At the same time, capacity reductions could challenge expectations for steady revenue growth and margin expansion if lost traffic and compensation costs outweigh any pricing or efficiency benefits.
  • The current narrative emphasizes fleet modernization and digitalization, but may not fully reflect the operational impact of a prolonged jet-fuel supply squeeze or the risk that similar cuts by peers like Air France KLM and IAG reshape competitive dynamics on key European and long haul corridors.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Deutsche Lufthansa to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Prolonged jet-fuel shortages and high prices could pressure Lufthansa’s cost base and force further capacity actions just as the sector enters peak travel months.
  • ⚠️ Large scale cancellations raise the risk of customer dissatisfaction, rebooking costs, and potential loss of share to competitors such as Ryanair, easyJet, or other network carriers if reliability is questioned.
  • 🎁 Concentrating flying on higher yielding routes and aircraft types could support unit revenue and help Lufthansa prioritize cash generation over sheer volume.
  • 🎁 Industry wide cuts across European airlines may limit oversupply, which can help support pricing discipline and reduce the risk of an aggressive capacity-driven fare war.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on how Lufthansa adjusts its route mix, ticket pricing, and fuel hedging in response to the jet-fuel crunch. Watch commentary on load factors, unit revenue, and disruption costs to see whether fewer flights translate into healthier profitability or simply lower scale. It is also worth tracking how peers respond, because sector wide discipline versus aggressive share grabbing will shape Lufthansa’s room to price and its competitive position across core hubs.

data.title: Is Merck (MRK) Still Attractive After A 40% One Year Share Price Gain
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Merck's current share price reflects its true worth, this breakdown will help you connect the recent moves in the stock with what fundamentals and valuation models actually say.
  • Merck recently closed at US$111.90, with a 40.2% return over the last year, alongside shorter term moves of a 6.0% decline over 7 days, a 6.5% decline over 30 days, and a 5.1% gain year to date.
  • Recent share price moves have put more attention on how market expectations line up with Merck's fundamentals and long term prospects. Evergreen coverage like this aims to give you a steadier view that is not tied to one specific headline or short term news cycle.
  • On Simply Wall St's valuation checks, Merck scores 5 out of 6. The sections that follow will compare different valuation approaches and then finish with a broader way to think about what that score really means for long term investors.

Merck delivered 40.2% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Pharmaceuticals industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Merck Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return, giving a single estimate of what the whole business could be worth now.

For Merck, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $12.6b. Analyst estimates and extrapolated figures suggest free cash flow of around $23.2b in 2030, with a path of projected cash flows between these two points and beyond, as shown in the ten year projection set provided.

When those future cash flows are discounted back, Simply Wall St’s model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of $214.74 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $111.90, this DCF output indicates that the stock is trading at roughly a 47.9% discount to that estimate, which the model interprets as undervalued on this cash flow basis.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Merck is undervalued by 47.9%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 54 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Merck.

Approach 2: Merck Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Merck, the P/E ratio is a useful way to link what you pay for the stock to the earnings the business is currently generating. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars investors are paying for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher risk typically line up with a lower P/E.

Merck currently trades on a P/E of 15.1x. That sits close to the Pharmaceuticals industry average of 15.9x and below the peer group average of 25.3x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio framework estimates a P/E of 27.8x for Merck, based on factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market value and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple industry or peer comparison because it adjusts the expected multiple for Merck’s own growth profile, risk characteristics and profitability, rather than assuming all companies in the group deserve similar pricing.

Since the Fair Ratio of 27.8x is materially higher than the current P/E of 15.1x, this approach suggests Merck’s shares are trading below that implied level.

Result: UNDERVALUED

A more cautious Narrative might sit closer to the lower fair value of about US$101.30, using revenue growth closer to 3.1%, profit margins only nudging up to about 28.7%, earnings nearer US$20.4b and a future P/E around 14.3x.

Bgin Blockchain (BGIN) FY 2025 earnings snapshot

Bgin Blockchain (BGIN) opened FY 2025 with first half revenue of about US$47.7 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.56, setting a cautious tone for the latest results. The company has seen revenue move from US$144.5 million and EPS of US$0.85 in the first half of FY 2024 to US$157.8 million and EPS of US$0.02 in the second half. Trailing twelve month figures show revenue of roughly US$205.5 million against a net loss of US$176.9 million and EPS of US$1.62 in losses. The key question for investors now is whether management can steady margins and contain further earnings pressure.

See our full analysis for Bgin Blockchain.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the most common narratives around Bgin Blockchain to see which stories still hold up and which are challenged by the margin profile.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

US$60 million first half loss reverses prior profitability

  • After reporting net income of US$63.4 million in the first half of FY 2024 and US$2.5 million in the second half, Bgin Blockchain posted a net loss of about US$60.4 million in the first half of FY 2025 on revenue of roughly US$47.7 million.
  • Critics highlight a bearish concern that profitability has not held up, and the figures line up with that view, as net income of US$65.9 million on trailing twelve month revenue of about US$302.3 million in the period ending the second half of FY 2024 has turned into a trailing twelve month loss of roughly US$176.9 million on revenue of US$205.5 million.
    • This shift also feeds into the reported 14.3% yearly increase in net losses over the past five years, which bearish investors point to as evidence of pressure on the earnings profile.
    • With basic EPS moving from US$0.84 and US$0.02 in the two halves of FY 2024 to a loss of about US$0.56 in the first half of FY 2025, the recent trend supports concerns that positive earnings have not been sustained.

Valuation premium with 5.4x P/S

  • Bgin Blockchain is trading on a P/S of 5.4x, which sits above both the reported US Tech industry average of 2.4x and the stated peer average of 0.5x, even though the company is currently unprofitable.
  • What stands out to bearish investors is that this richer multiple sits alongside losses, as trailing twelve month net income of about US$176.9 million in losses contrasts with the premium P/S, so any pressure on revenue of US$205.5 million could weigh heavily on how that valuation is viewed.
    • Bears argue that paying more than double the industry P/S for a company that has seen losses grow at about 14.3% per year over five years leaves little room for disappointment.
    • They also point to the peer group P/S of 0.5x as a reference, suggesting the current 5.4x level may be sensitive to any further weak EPS prints, such as the recent trailing twelve month loss per share of about US$1.62.

  • Bears argue that this mix of expanding losses and recent share price swings heightens risk, and the numbers provide several reference points, with EPS moving from a trailing twelve month profit of about US$0.61 per share in the period ending the second half of FY 2024 to a trailing twelve month loss of roughly US$1.62 per share alongside the reported volatility.
    • This pattern means anyone holding the stock needs to be comfortable with earnings that have moved between profit and loss within a short window while the share price has not been stable relative to the broader market.
    • For readers watching entry or exit levels, the combination of a US$3.19 share price, a richer 5.4x P/S, and growing losses is exactly what those with a bearish tilt reference when they talk about higher trading risk around each new set of numbers.

data.title: Be Sure To Check Out Astra Industrial Group Company (TADAWUL:1212) Before It Goes Ex-Dividend
data.description:
Astra Industrial Group Company (TADAWUL:1212) stock is about to trade ex-dividend in three days. The ex-dividend date is two business days before a company's record date in most cases, which is the date on which the company determines which shareholders are entitled to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is important because any transaction on a stock needs to have been settled before the record date in order to be eligible for a dividend. Meaning, you will need to purchase Astra Industrial Group's shares before the 30th of April to receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 7th of May.

The company's next dividend payment will be ر.س3.50 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of ر.س3.00 to shareholders. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that Astra Industrial Group has a trailing yield of 2.1% on the current share price of ر.س143.70. Dividends are an important source of income to many shareholders, but the health of the business is crucial to maintaining those dividends. That's why we should always check whether the dividend payments appear sustainable, and if the company is growing.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. Astra Industrial Group paid out a comfortable 37% of its profit last year. That said, even highly profitable companies sometimes might not generate enough cash to pay the dividend, which is why we should always check if the dividend is covered by cash flow. It distributed 37% of its free cash flow as dividends, a comfortable payout level for most companies.

It's positive to see that Astra Industrial Group's dividend is covered by both profits and cash flow, since this is generally a sign that the dividend is sustainable, and a lower payout ratio usually suggests a greater margin of safety before the dividend gets cut.

Check out our latest analysis for Astra Industrial Group

Click here to see how much of its profit Astra Industrial Group paid out over the last 12 months.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. It's encouraging to see Astra Industrial Group has grown its earnings rapidly, up 42% a year for the past five years. Astra Industrial Group is paying out less than half its earnings and cash flow, while simultaneously growing earnings per share at a rapid clip. Companies with growing earnings and low payout ratios are often the best long-term dividend stocks, as the company can both grow its earnings and increase the percentage of earnings that it pays out, essentially multiplying the dividend.

Many investors will assess a company's dividend performance by evaluating how much the dividend payments have changed over time. In the past 10 years, Astra Industrial Group has increased its dividend at approximately 16% a year on average. It's great to see earnings per share growing rapidly over several years, and dividends per share growing right along with it.

Final Takeaway

Is Astra Industrial Group an attractive dividend stock, or better left on the shelf? Astra Industrial Group has grown its earnings per share while simultaneously reinvesting in the business. Unfortunately it's cut the dividend at least once in the past 10 years, but the conservative payout ratio makes the current dividend look sustainable. It's a promising combination that should mark this company worthy of closer attention.

data.title: UBS Faces Swiss Capital Dispute And Reassesses Growth Versus Resilience
data.description:
• UBS Group (SWX:UBSG) is challenging new Swiss capital rules that it describes as extreme and harmful for the Swiss economy.
  • The Swiss government has finalized capital adequacy requirements that would add significant capital demands on UBS, including 100% capital backing for foreign units at the parent level.
  • UBS has publicly opposed these measures, arguing they are not aligned with international standards and could affect the bank's global operations.

UBS is one of the largest global wealth managers and investment banks, with a balance sheet that spans multiple regions and business lines. Tighter Swiss capital rules arrive at a time when large banks face closer scrutiny of resilience, resolution planning, and cross border exposures. For investors, this kind of regulatory shift speaks directly to how a bank funds itself, prices risk, and allocates capital across businesses.

In the future, the dispute around capital requirements could influence how UBS sets priorities between growth, shareholder returns, and balance sheet strength. As you assess SWX:UBSG, some key considerations are how any final rules might affect its financial flexibility, booking structure, and willingness to keep certain activities or entities in Switzerland compared with other jurisdictions.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for UBS Group by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on UBS Group.

Is UBS Group's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis.

The new Swiss capital rules matter for UBS because they go straight to how much common equity the bank must lock up against its global operations. UBS has indicated that the overall package, including earlier incremental demands linked to the Credit Suisse deal, could translate into roughly $37b of additional CET1 capital. Requirements such as 100% capital backing of foreign units at the parent level can affect how attractive it is to book activities in Switzerland, compared with hubs like London, New York or Frankfurt where peers such as Credit Suisse’s former rivals, Deutsche Bank or BNP Paribas, face different rule sets. For you, the key questions are whether higher buffers eventually push UBS toward a lower return profile, a different legal structure for foreign subsidiaries, or changes in how it funds and prices higher risk businesses.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The UBS Group Narrative

  • The tougher capital package directly links to narrative themes around rising regulatory burdens and higher structural costs. This reinforces the idea that regulation is a central driver of UBS’s long-term earnings path.
  • If UBS needs to hold more capital than anticipated, that could challenge expectations in the narrative that margin expansion and earnings growth will come through cleanly from Credit Suisse integration and digital investment.
  • The final calibration of Swiss rules, including the timing and phase in of any extra buffers, may not be fully reflected in existing narrative assumptions that were built before the ordinance was finalized.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for UBS Group to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Higher required capital, potentially up to an extra $37b of CET1, could weigh on return on equity and limit flexibility for acquisitions, lending growth or larger buybacks.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 3 key risks for UBS, including regulatory pressure and integration complexity, which could be amplified if Swiss rules force faster or larger balance sheet adjustments than planned.
  • 🎁 UBS’s position as a global wealth manager with diversified fee income can help absorb regulatory costs compared with more narrowly focused investment banking peers.
  • 🎁 If UBS adapts its structure and booking model efficiently, higher capital could support a stronger perceived resilience profile, which may be attractive for clients and funding counterparties.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, watch how UBS quantifies the impact of the new ordinance when it updates the market, especially any guidance on additional CET1 needs, targeted return on equity, and capital return plans. Signals about potential changes to the group structure, such as where activities are booked or how foreign subsidiaries are capitalized, will also be important. Finally, monitor comments from Swiss authorities on timing and possible fine tuning of the rules, as any flexibility on phase in periods or calibration could influence how heavy the near term financial impact is for UBS.

Approach 1: Ciena Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes projected future cash flows and discounts them back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now. It focuses on the cash the company could generate for shareholders rather than on earnings multiples.

For Ciena, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $768.4 million. Analysts provide explicit forecasts for several years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates further out to build a 10 year path, including a projected free cash flow of about $788.2 million in 2030. Each of these future cash flows is discounted back to today and summed.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/S multiple usually reflects what investors expect for growth and how much risk they see. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk tend to support higher multiples, while lower growth or higher risk usually point to lower multiples.
